title: Spontaneous Parasitic Order: Predatory Publishing as Extended Phenotype
description:
Empirical corpus and theoretical framework for analyzing predatory academic journal invitations

This repository contains:

  • 8 documented invitation cases (7 predatory, 1 legitimate control)
  • Parasitic Spontaneous Order (PSO) Index methodology
  • Python analysis tools for pattern identification
  • Extended Phenotype Theory application to institutional analysis
  • Complete documentation and case studies

Key Contribution: Reframes predatory publishing as evolutionarily stable institutional parasitism, not isolated fraud. Applies Richard Dawkins' Extended Phenotype Theory and F.A. Hayek's spontaneous order concepts to explain emergence, stability, and diversification of predatory journals.

Empirical Findings: PSO Index (0-5 scale) achieves 100% classification accuracy across corpus. Six systematic patterns identified: disciplinary mismatch, mimetic legitimacy, emotional hooks, friction minimization, automation indicators, and financial extraction.

Policy Implications: Shifts focus from reactive blacklisting to proactive incentive modification.
references: Dawkins, R. (1982). The Extended Phenotype: The Long Reach of the Gene. Oxford University Press.
Hayek, F. A. (1973). Law, Legislation and Liberty, Volume 1: Rules and Order. University of Chicago Press.
Beall, J. (2012). Predatory publishers are corrupting open access. Nature, 489(7415), 179.
Grudniewicz, A., et al. (2019). Predatory journals: no definition, no defence. Nature, 576(7786), 210-212.
